edge: Concept learning involves the use of a model that balances tractability of inference against the expressivity of the hypothesis class.
Humans can efficiently learn a broad range of concepts, which is a core tension in models of concept learning.
The introduced model of inductive learning aims to be human-like in its approach to concept learning.
The introduced model implements a Bayesian reasoning process for proposing candidate hypotheses expressed in natural language.
The inductive learning model proposes candidate hypotheses expressed in natural language to imitate human learning.
The language model re-weighs the candidate hypotheses using a prior and a likelihood.
The prior of the Bayesian reasoning process is estimated from human data to predict human judgments on learning problems involving numbers and sets.
The process predicts human judgments on learning problems by estimating the prior from human data.
Humans are efficient in learning a broad range of concepts, which necessitates models of concept learning to account for this human-like learning ability.
An intelligent system is challenged to acquire a wide range of new concepts given modest data and computational resources.
Building AI systems that can efficiently master many concepts is practically valuable for data-efficiency and broad generalization.
The problem of induction involves inferring a generalizable pattern, rule, trend, or law from specific examples.
The Bayesian paradigm starts with a hypothesis space of possible concepts to probabilistically infer which hypothesis most likely generated the observed data using Bayes’ Rule.
The Bayesian paradigm has proved widely applicable across both cognitive science and artificial intelligence.
Many Bayesian models design hypothesis spaces that resemble a programming language for increased expressivity.

The model differs from the baseline in two key ways - it uses first-order primitives that can express concepts not in the dataset and supports efficient inference via bottom-up proposals compared to baseline's stochastic search over  hypotheses.
The model can express concepts like objects with the least common color and explains 81% of the variance in human responses, which is much higher than GPT-4.
The baseline model performs a stochastic search over hypotheses (MCMC) requiring many more samples compared to the model's efficient inference via bottom-up proposals.
The model's predictions align well with human judgments and fit the human data somewhat better than the BPL baseline, even with an exorbitant sampling budget.

Our model correctly predicts that humans will learn the concept of majority color after just 2 batches of examples. It predicts learning minority color after 4 batches, while humans need just 3, but the primary result—that this concept is very learnable—holds for both humans and the model.

Although these two new concepts are easy to explain in words, and could be expressed in first-order logic with the right primitives—set cardinality and quantification over colors—neither concepts are learnable by the BPL baseline.
This is because both concepts are simply unrepresentable: despite being equipped with 28 primitives, those primitives were designed without anticipating these new concepts.
This highlights the fact that it is difficult to preprogram a sufficiently broad set of primitives.
